secsa__001_a.text:
Domestic abuse (DA) affects many women over the age of 60 and has a profound effect on their well-
being. Older women face distinct and often overlooked barriers to seeking help, such as ageist attitudes,
stigma and assumptions that DA does not happen in later life. Older women are more likely to be
financially dependent or live with the perpetrator, making it harder to access support and cope with the
abuse. Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing (PATH) is a trauma-informed intervention delivered by
DA practitioners that improves psychological outcomes in younger women. However, its effectiveness
for older women remains unclear.
In this programme of research, our aim is to adapt the PATH programme (A-PATH) to meet the unique
needs of older women, and assess its feasibility and acceptability for a future large scale randomised
controlled trial (RCT).
We will address two key research questions:
a) is it possible to adapt PATH in a way that is accessible to a diverse range of older women affected by
DA to empower and support their wellbeing, and recovery?
b) is it feasible and acceptable to deliver A-PATH in primary care, and community settings?
Our mixed-methods programme of researc

secsa__001_b.text:
h will include three interlinked Work Streams, with a Lived
Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) contributing throughout.
Work Stream 1: We will conduct two survivor-led focus groups (n = 16) with older women affected by DA
to tailor the components of PATH to better meet the needs of older women. Practitioner focus groups (n
= 10) will further inform the adaptation of PATH, help identify training needs, and enhance practitioners’
ability to recognise the needs of older women.
Work Stream 2: Using individual interviews with survivors (n=10), and shadowing helpline phone calls
(n=10) through our partner Hourglass, we will explore the barriers older women face in accessing
support. We will work with frontline professionals and clinicians (n = 15) to identify and develop referral
pathways, that improve visibility and accessibility of services for older women in both primary care and
community settings.
Work Stream 3: We will test the feasibility and acceptability of A-PATH in a pilot RCT (n = 76 women),
by randomly allocating 38 women to DA advocacy and 38 to DA advocacy plus A-PATH. We will
measure rates of recruitment, randomisation, retention, and participant engagement with the
interve

secsa__001_c.text:
ntion. We will identify and administer measures which we think may be suitable as primary and
secondary outcomes in a full trial at baseline, 4, 8, and 12 months. This will include: psychological
distress, depression, and post-traumatic stress. We will assess the acceptability of collecting health-
related quality of life data to inform a future economic evaluation and conduct interviews with older
women and practitioners to explore lived experiences and contextual factors influencing outcomes.
We will work closely with community providers to engage women affected by multiple inequalities and
explore how these intersecting factors influence access to support services. Findings will be shared
through a series of accessible reports tailored for diverse audiences, alongside a public engagement
event to communicate key learning. If feasible, this study will provide the foundation for a future large
scale RCT evaluating the effectiveness of the adapted PATH, generating much needed evidence to
improve trauma-informed care and support for older women affected by DA.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Background to the research
Some women over the age of 60 are living with domestic abuse, which means they are being hurt or
mistreated by someone close to them, such as a partner or ex-partner. This abuse can include physical
harm, made to feel scared or upset, being controlled, or stopped from making their own choices.
Sometimes, this abuse lasts for many years and may involve emotional, sexual, or financial harm. Most
domestic abuse support services are aimed at younger women and may not fully understand or meet
the needs of older women especially those who are still living with the person causing them harm. This
can make it much harder for older women to get the help they need to stay safe.
Aim of the research
Our research aims to help provide better support for older women who are living with abuse. Women
who have lived through domestic abuse have told us that they want a safe and supportive space to
share memories of what happened and begin planning for their future. In this project, we will work with a
small group of older women to find out if a programme called ‘Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing’
(PATH) can help them stay safe and feel better. PATH has already been show
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
n to help younger women
(under 60) who have experienced abuse.
Research plan
We will work with older women and their supporters to make the PATH programme better suited to their
needs. We will learn about the challenges they face when seeking help and work closely with health and
support workers to find the easiest ways for them to get the support they need. We will invite a group of
older women to take part in a study to try out the PATH programme. The group will be split into two
smaller groups at random. This means no one will choose which group they are in; it will be decided by
chance. One group will be offered the usual support offered to survivors of domestic abuse, such as
meetings with a trained support worker. The other group will get this same support, plus weekly one-to-
one therapy sessions for 8 to 12 weeks. This will help us understand whether PATH can benefit older
women and what changes may be needed to make it more suitable. At the end of the study, we will ask
women in both groups to share their thoughts about what worked well and what could be better, to help
plan the next steps.
Working with diverse people and communities
Older women of different ages and backg
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
rounds who have lived experience of abuse, have played a key
role in shaping the project, and will be involved throughout to ensure the programme meets everyone’s
needs. An older woman researcher with personal experience of domestic abuse is working closely with
us to make sure the study truly reflects what support older women need. The national charity Hourglass,
which works to end the abuse of older people, will help our study to ensure it is shaped by the needs
and voices of older women.
Knowledge mobilisation
When the study is finished, our public contributors will guide us in sharing what we have learned with the
women we worked with, as well as with doctors, nurses and organisations that support women affected
by abuse, including those who work with older women. If this small study testing the A-PATH
programme shows it could be helpful for older women, we will apply for further funding to carry out a
larger study with more people. Later, we hope to work with policymakers to help bring lasting
improvements in the well-being and safety of older women harmed by domestic abuse.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

seccfps__001_a.text:
Thank you for these very helpful comments. Below is our detailed plan of all changes for Stage 2 based
on your feedback:
1. The applicants should describe how retention will be investigated in the feasibility study.
Thank you for this helpful comment which has been addressed. Stop/go criteria are now specified (see
section primary outcomes). Retention will be a key focus of our feasibility randomised controlled trial. As
described in our full proposal, we will collect both quantitative and qualitative data to assess retention.
This mixed-methods approach will enable us to assess the feasibility of achieving adequate retention
(see pages 7-9).
2. The applicants should describe how they will be encouraging recruitment from non-English speaking
and ethnic communities.
Thank you for this very important comment. As described in page 18 to encourage recruitment from non-
English speaking and ethnically diverse communities, we will implement inclusive and culturally sensitive
engagement strategies throughout the study. Recruitment materials (i.e. participant information sheets,
posters) will be reviewed for cultural appropriateness. We will work in partnership with frontline services
that
seccfps__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_b.text:
have established trust within ethnic minority communities to raise awareness of the study. Involving
community representatives in the co-design of recruitment strategies ensures they are responsive to
cultural needs. These measures will foster equitable access and participation from under-represented
groups, ultimately improving the relevance of the adapted PATH intervention.
3. The applicants should correct the timeline for workstream 3.
Thank you for pointing out this error. This has now been corrected.
4. The committee requires reassurance that the study can be delivered within the proposed time and
budget.
Thank you for your valuable feedback. We are confident in delivering the study within the proposed
timeframe and budget, supported by our proven track record. Our team has extensive experience
managing complex intervention studies in areas such as dementia, end-of-life care, and domestic
abuse, having led multiple trials with similar deadlines. We have strong partnerships with key
organisations (e.g., Hourglass, Women’s Aid, Age UK, SafeLives) that will actively support recruitment.
We will work closely with NHS Trusts that have demonstrated leadership in domestic abuse rese
seccfps__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_c.text:
arch
and practice (e.g., Camden and Islington NHS Trust – Pathfinder Project; Central and North West
London NHS Foundation Trust – routine screening implementation) to strengthen recruitment through
collaboration and shared learning. The project includes continuous monitoring and oversight to keep the
study on track and within budget, maximising quality and impact. Following recommendations for
involving underserved groups, we have expanded to ten sites and a recruitment period of eight
months—an approach shown to improve diversity and make study findings more relevant and impactful.
5. The applicants should describe the intervention development.
Thank you for this comment. We have now described the development of A-PATH in detail in section
Work Stream 1. The development of the intervention will follow established best practice for complex
intervention development (drawing from the Medical Research Council framework). It will involve a
phased, collaborative approach with experts by experience, DA practitioners, clinicians and third sector
to ensure it is relevant, acceptable, and tailored to the needs of older women affected by DA.
6. The plain English summary should be rewritten
seccfps__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_d.text:
for a general audience.
Thank you for this helpful feedback. We have revised the Plain English Summary to ensure it is clear,
and accessible. The revised version now explains in lay terms the purpose of the study, what the
research will involve, and how it could benefit older women affected by DA. We have involved members
of our Public and Patient Involvement (PPI) group, in reviewing the revised summary to ensure it is
understandable to a general audience. Our plain language summary has been developed with input
from our DA practitioner co-applicant, NIHR research design lay members, and experts by experience
consulted through our partner organisation, Hourglass.
7. The applicants should clarify the rationale for the 4-month follow-up being the main outcome
measurement point.
Thank you for this very important feedback. We consulted with lived experience experts to inform our
choice of primary outcome timepoint. They strongly emphasised the need to allow sufficient time for
meaningful and sustainable change to emerge given the complex, long-standing nature of DA in later
life. Their insights highlighted ensuring participants have adequate time to complete A-PATH and begin
applying
seccfps__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_e.text:
its content to their daily lives. This longer timeframe aligns with other DA intervention RCTs,
and NIHR feasibility study guidance, which support the importance of assessing medium-term impacts.
For the future full-scale trial, we will extend follow-up to 12 months as our primary outcome timepoint,
consistent with the approach used in our REPROVIDE trial, to enable measurement of longer-term
recovery outcomes.
8. The applicants should clearly describe the definition of adherence that they intend to use in the study.
We now describe the definition of adherence that we will use in our feasibility study. This is based on our
previous PATH trial, prior research in the area, our lived experience study, and feedback by our PPI
group. Details of our approach to adherence is described in detail in page 9.
9. The applicants should give more detail about sample size calculations.
We now expand on our sample size justification (see Work Stream 3, pages 8-9). This is based on
guidance for feasibility trials where the focus is on estimating parameters such as recruitment, retention,
and adherence with sufficient precision (NIHR RfPB Guidance, 2023).
seccfps__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

secppi__001_a.text:
We define our community as women aged 60 and over who have experienced domestic abuse, along
with their families, supporters, and the organisations that contribute to their recovery and well-being. Our
community-engaged research approach is grounded in the principles of equity, inclusivity, shared
learning, and collaborative decision-making, bringing together lived experience experts, practitioners,
third sector partners, clinicians and researchers.
We will work in close partnership with older women and the professionals who support them to co-
produce knowledge and promote meaningful involvement in all stages of the research. Our approach will
ensure that older women’s voices are central to shaping the intervention, and improving referral
pathways. We will evaluate the strength of our community engagement, by monitoring and measuring:
(1) the number and diversity of community contributors (e.g., local services, third sector organisations,
gatekeepers);
(2) engagement across the study, noting retention and consistency of community voices in shaping
study procedures, materials, and dissemination plans;
(3) the perceived impact of involvement, assessed through follow-up interviews wi

secppi__001_b.text:
th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by
dome

secppi__001_c.text:
stic abuse.
We are committed to ensuring mutual benefit in all aspects of community involvement. Contributions will
be recognised through participation vouchers, flexible scheduling, and practical support to reduce
barriers such as digital exclusion. We will also offer lived experience experts opportunities for training in
research to support their ongoing development and meaningful involvement in the study (through
training in the PATH model and qualitative research methods).
Community members will receive letters of recognition to support future engagement in research. Our
LEAP, led by our peer researcher, will take a lead role in co-producing plain language summaries of our
findings for community dissemination. At the end of the project, we will host a public engagement event
with our partners (Hourglass, Age UK, Women’s Aid) to share results, raise awareness, and foster wider
social change. Rooted in co-production, this study will prioritise safe, inclusive participation to ensure the
research remains relevant, and respectful to the communities it serves.

secsb__001_a.text: Staff Costs 1. Chief Investigators and Co-Applicant Team Dr Vasiliki Orgeta (Lead for WS1 and WS3) and Dr Helen Cramer (Lead for WS2) will have overall responsibility for the project’s delivery and management. Dr Orgeta will lead recruitment at the London sites and oversee recruitment across all sites (costed at 10% FTE). She will manage the NHS Ethics and HRA approvals, supported by colleagues at Noclor and the UCLH/UCL Joint Research Office. She will lead all site initiation meetings with all recruitment centres during the set-up phase (see Timeplan; Months 0–9) to ensure timely and coordinated study delivery. She will also engage community-based hubs to support study recruitment and outreach. This hands-on approach will support smooth operational start-up and foster strong collaborative relationships critical to recruitment success for each site. She is costed at 10% for the first 30 months, and at 5% for the last 6 months of the study. Dr Cramer will lead recruitment at the Bristol site and will lead WS2, focused on developing referral pathways (costed at 10% FTE for 30 months and 5% for the remaining 6 months). Professor Sarah Wydall (Swansea site lead, WS3 Co-Lead, co-applica

secsb__001_b.text: nt) will oversee recruitment at the Swansea site and co-lead the WS3 qualitative process evaluation of lived experiences for survivors and practitioners (costed at 7% FTE for 24 months). Dr Roxane Agnew-Davies (co-applicant, Co-Lead of WS1) will lead on A-PATH training and supervision, contributing to the adaptation of training and A- PATH materials (costed for 57 days). Dr Alison Gregory (co-applicant, WS2 Co-Lead) will support the development of community referral pathways (WS2) and lead on third sector engagement partnerships with Women’s Aid and SafeLives (costed for 40 days). Dr Victoria Vickerstaff (statistical and health economics lead; co-applicant) will oversee statistical and economic evaluation (costed at 7.5% FTE for 24 months), supported by a junior researcher (employed by UCL). Marion Goodchild (lived experience co-applicant) will lead the PPI strategy and chair the Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). She will guide the adaptation of PATH, advise on recruitment, and review all public-facing materials (costed for 20 days). 2. Research Staff Employed by UCL We will employ a senior researcher (50% FTE for 22 months and 25% for 10 months; Grade 7) with a background in

secsb__001_c.text: social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. Total Staff Costs: £168,895.96 Dr Claire Hawcroft (Honorary Researcher, Univers

secsb__001_d.text: ity of Bristol) will lead on the distress protocol and advise on primary care referral pathways. Amanda Warburton (Honorary Researcher, UCL Psychiatry) will oversee recruitment of DA practitioners, develop the safeguarding protocol, and lead on safeguarding procedures throughout the trial. Non-Staff Costs 1. Data Capture, Randomisation, Training, and Participant Vouchers We have allocated costs for randomisation via Sealed Envelope (100 randomisations). REDCap will be used for data capture (annual fee waived for UCL). We have included training costs for our key outcome measures, and the fee for registering our trial (ISRCTN). All participants will receive £25 vouchers as recognition for their contribution. Total: £5,886.40 2. Dissemination We have included costs for a podcast to be co-produced with UCL Media, a final dissemination event, and attendance at a national conference. Total: £7,360.00 3. SoECAT Site-Level Research Costs As outlined in the SoECAT, these include research activities essential to study delivery, including recruitment, consent, and data collection. Total: £10,218.08 4. Estate, Infrastructure Technician Costs, and Other Indirect costs These amount to £143,023.4

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application describes a clear research vision for adapting the PATH intervention for older women (g.1.a) and outlines a distinctive approach involving a Lived Experience Advisory Panel (g.1.b). There is evidence of institutional support via the Priment CTU and mentorship for a junior researcher (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application is a project grant, not a career development award; therefore, it lacks a personal career trajectory or specific training plan for an individual applicant (g.1.a, g.1.c). The 'distinctive ideas' are project-level innovations rather than an independent research direction for a specific person (g.1.b). Mentorship is described for a staff member, not a primary applicant's career development (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.rationale: The application is a project grant, not a career development award; therefore, it lacks a personal career trajectory or specific training plan for an individual applicant (g.1.a, g.1.c). The 'distinctive ideas' are project-level innovations rather than an independent research direction for a specific person (g.1.b). Mentorship is described for a staff member, not a primary applicant's career development (g.1.d).

evidence.text: social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The Application Context lists team members with prior experience in NIHR-funded clinical trials in dementia, domestic abuse, and end-of-life care (g.3.a). The team's experience aligns well with the skills required for the proposed mixed-methods feasibility study (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application does not mention specific formal training courses or certifications for the applicants (g.3.b). The evidence of experience is at the team level rather than for a specific individual applicant, which is atypical for this rubric section.
sub_criteria.rationale: The application does not mention specific formal training courses or certifications for the applicants (g.3.b). The evidence of experience is at the team level rather than for a specific individual applicant, which is atypical for this rubric section.

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY (IP) FEEDBACK:
Is Background IP owned by someone other than the Contractor (a Third Party) used in the delivery of the research? No Are any items of Foreground IP intended to rest somewhere other than the Contractor? No Other changes to the application No other changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

evidence.text: Staff Costs 1. Chief Investigators and Co-Applicant Team Dr Vasiliki Orgeta (Lead for WS1 and WS3) and Dr Helen Cramer (Lead for WS2) will have overall responsibility for the project’s delivery and management. Dr Orgeta will lead recruitment at the London sites and oversee recruitment across all sites (costed at 10% FTE). She will manage the NHS Ethics and HRA approvals, supported by colleagues at Noclor and the UCLH/UCL Joint Research Office. She will lead all site initiation meetings with all recruitment centres during the set-up phase (see Timeplan; Months 0–9) to ensure timely and coordinated study delivery. She will also engage community-based hubs to support study recruitment and outreach. This hands-on approach will support smooth operational start-up and foster strong collaborative relationships critical to recruitment success for each site. She is costed at 10% for the first 30 months, and at 5% for the last 6 months of the study. Dr Cramer will lead recruitment at the Bristol site and will lead WS2, focused on developing referral pathways (costed at 10% FTE for 30 months and 5% for the remaining 6 months). FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application describes a clear research vision for adapting the PATH intervention for older women (g.1.a) and outlines a distinctive approach involving a Lived Experience Advisory Panel (g.1.b). There is evidence of institutional support via the Priment CTU and mentorship for a junior researcher (g.1.d).
criteria.drawbacks: The application is a project grant, not a career development award; therefore, it lacks a personal career trajectory or specific training plan for an individual applicant (g.1.a, g.1.c). The 'distinctive ideas' are project-level innovations rather than an independent research direction for a specific person (g.1.b). Mentorship is described for a staff member, not a primary applicant's career development (g.1.d).
criteria.rationale: The application is a project grant, not a career development award; therefore, it lacks a personal career trajectory or specific training plan for an individual applicant (g.1.a, g.1.c). The 'distinctive ideas' are project-level innovations rather than an independent research direction for a specific person (g.1.b). Mentorship is described for a staff member, not a primary applicant's career development (g.1.d).

This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

No Are any items of Foreground IP intended to rest somewhere other than the Contractor? No Other changes to the application No other changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

criteria.pros: The Application Context lists team members with prior experience in NIHR-funded clinical trials in dementia, domestic abuse, and end-of-life care (g.3.a). The team's experience aligns well with the skills required for the proposed mixed-methods feasibility study (g.3.c).
criteria.drawbacks: The application does not mention specific formal training courses or certifications for the applicants (g.3.b). The evidence of experience is at the team level rather than for a specific individual applicant, which is atypical for this rubric section.
criteria.rationale: The application does not mention specific formal training courses or certifications for the applicants (g.3.b). The evidence of experience is at the team level rather than for a specific individual applicant, which is atypical for this rubric section.
