FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application clearly defines supervisory roles for Dr. Orgeta and Dr. Cramer, including specific responsibilities for recruitment and management (secsb__001_a). It details a structured training plan and mentorship for a senior researcher (secsb__001_c) and mentions the lead applicant joining the Priment CTU Steering Group for ongoing support (secac__001).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While roles and mentorship are described, the specific frequency or format of supervision meetings (e.g., weekly, monthly) is not explicitly stated, limiting the evidence for a fully structured supervision framework.
sub_criteria.rationale: While roles and mentorship are described, the specific frequency or format of supervision meetings (e.g., weekly, monthly) is not explicitly stated, limiting the evidence for a fully structured supervision framework.

evidence.text: Staff Costs 1. Chief Investigators and Co-Applicant Team Dr Vasiliki Orgeta (Lead for WS1 and WS3) and Dr Helen Cramer (Lead for WS2) will have overall responsibility for the project’s delivery and management. Dr Orgeta will lead recruitment at the London sites and oversee recruitment across all sites (costed at 10% FTE). She will manage the NHS Ethics and HRA approvals, supported by colleagues at Noclor and the UCLH/UCL Joint Research Office. She will lead all site initiation meetings with all recruitment centres during the set-up phase (see Timeplan; Months 0–9) to ensure timely and coordinated study delivery. She will also engage community-based hubs to support study recruitment and outreach. This hands-on approach will support smooth operational start-up and foster strong collaborative relationships critical to recruitment success for each site. She is costed at 10% for the first 30 months, and at 5% for the last 6 months of the study. Dr Cramer will lead recruitment at the Bristol site and will lead WS2, focused on developing referral pathways (costed at 10% FTE for 30 months and 5% for the remaining 6 months). Professor Sarah Wydall (Swansea site lead, WS3 Co-Lead, co-applica

nt) will oversee recruitment at the Swansea site and co-lead the WS3 qualitative process evaluation of lived experiences for survivors and practitioners (costed at 7% FTE for 24 months). Dr Roxane Agnew-Davies (co-applicant, Co-Lead of WS1) will lead on A-PATH training and supervision, contributing to the adaptation of training and A- PATH materials (costed for 57 days). Dr Alison Gregory (co-applicant, WS2 Co-Lead) will support the development of community referral pathways (WS2) and lead on third sector engagement partnerships with Women’s Aid and SafeLives (costed for 40 days). Dr Victoria Vickerstaff (statistical and health economics lead; co-applicant) will oversee statistical and economic evaluation (costed at 7.5% FTE for 24 months), supported by a junior researcher (employed by UCL). Marion Goodchild (lived experience co-applicant) will lead the PPI strategy and chair the Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). She will guide the adaptation of PATH, advise on recruitment, and review all public-facing materials (costed for 20 days). 2. Research Staff Employed by UCL We will employ a senior researcher (50% FTE for 22 months and 25% for 10 months; Grade 7) with a background in

social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The budget explicitly allocates costs for estate, infrastructure, and technician support (secsb__001_d), demonstrating available resources. The application confirms access to UCL's Priment Clinical Trials Unit for study design and delivery guidance (secac__001), directly linking organizational support to project success.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly detail how the organizational focus aligns with the specific career trajectory of the applicant (as this is a project grant rather than a training award, this is less critical but still a gap in the specific signal definition). Tailored support specific to the award type is implied through the CTU but not explicitly framed as 'award-specific' support.

ity of Bristol) will lead on the distress protocol and advise on primary care referral pathways. Amanda Warburton (Honorary Researcher, UCL Psychiatry) will oversee recruitment of DA practitioners, develop the safeguarding protocol, and lead on safeguarding procedures throughout the trial. Non-Staff Costs 1. Data Capture, Randomisation, Training, and Participant Vouchers We have allocated costs for randomisation via Sealed Envelope (100 randomisations). REDCap will be used for data capture (annual fee waived for UCL). We have included training costs for our key outcome measures, and the fee for registering our trial (ISRCTN). All participants will receive £25 vouchers as recognition for their contribution. Total: £5,886.40 2. Dissemination We have included costs for a podcast to be co-produced with UCL Media, a final dissemination event, and attendance at a national conference. Total: £7,360.00 3. SoECAT Site-Level Research Costs As outlined in the SoECAT, these include research activities essential to study delivery, including recruitment, consent, and data collection. Total: £10,218.08 4. Estate, Infrastructure Technician Costs, and Other Indirect costs These amount to £143,023.4

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a commitment to inclusivity through specific recruitment strategies for diverse groups (secac__001) and the inclusion of a PPIE co-applicant leading the engagement strategy (secsb__001_b). There is evidence of inclusive recruitment initiatives via partnerships with community organizations.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: There is no explicit mention of organizational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or adherence to research integrity principles at the institutional level. The evidence is largely project-specific rather than reflecting a broader organizational culture or specific training initiatives for underrepresented groups within the institution.
sub_criteria.rationale: There is no explicit mention of organizational policies promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or adherence to research integrity principles at the institutional level. The evidence is largely project-specific rather than reflecting a broader organizational culture or specific training initiatives for underrepresented groups within the institution.

evidence.text: social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs

criteria.pros: The application clearly defines supervisory roles for Dr. Orgeta and Dr. Cramer, including specific responsibilities for recruitment and management (secsb__001_a). criteria.drawbacks: While roles and mentorship are described, the specific frequency or format of supervision meetings (e.g., weekly, monthly) is not explicitly stated, limiting the evidence for a fully structured supervision framework.

evidence.text: Staff Costs 1. Chief Investigators and Co-Applicant Team Dr Vasiliki Orgeta (Lead for WS1 and WS3) and Dr Helen Cramer (Lead for WS2) will have overall responsibility for the project’s delivery and management. Dr Orgeta will lead recruitment at the London sites and oversee recruitment across all sites (costed at 10% FTE). She will manage the NHS Ethics and HRA approvals, supported by colleagues at Noclor and the UCLH/UCL Joint Research Office. She will lead all site initiation meetings with all recruitment centres during the set-up phase (see Timeplan; Months 0–9) to ensure timely and coordinated study delivery. She will also engage community-based hubs to support study recruitment and outreach. This hands-on approach will support smooth operational start-up and foster strong collaborative relationships critical to recruitment success for each site. She is costed at 10% for the first 30 months, and at 5% for the last 6 months of the study. Dr Cramer will lead recruitment at the Bristol site and will lead WS2, focused on developing referral pathways (costed at 10% FTE for 30 months and 5% for the remaining 6 months). Professor Sarah Wydall (Swansea site lead, WS3 Co-Lead, co-applica

nt) will oversee recruitment at the Swansea site and co-lead the WS3 qualitative process evaluation of lived experiences for survivors and practitioners (costed at 7% FTE for 24 months). Dr Roxane Agnew-Davies (co-applicant, Co-Lead of WS1) will lead on A-PATH training and supervision, contributing to the adaptation of training and A- PATH materials (costed for 57 days). Dr Alison Gregory (co-applicant, WS2 Co-Lead) will support the development of community referral pathways (WS2) and lead on third sector engagement partnerships with Women’s Aid and SafeLives (costed for 40 days). Dr Victoria Vickerstaff (statistical and health economics lead; co-applicant) will oversee statistical and economic evaluation (costed at 7.5% FTE for 24 months), supported by a junior researcher (employed by UCL). Marion Goodchild (lived experience co-applicant) will lead the PPI strategy and chair the Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). She will guide the adaptation of PATH, advise on recruitment, and review all public-facing materials (costed for 20 days). 2. Research Staff Employed by UCL We will employ a senior researcher (50% FTE for 22 months and 25% for 10 months; Grade 7) with a background in

social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

criteria.pros: The budget explicitly allocates costs for estate, infrastructure, and technician support (secsb__001_d), demonstrating available resources. The application confirms access to UCL's Priment Clinical Trials Unit for study design and delivery guidance (secac__001), directly linking organizational support to project success.
criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly detail how the organizational focus aligns with the specific career trajectory of the applicant (as this is a project grant rather than a training award, this is less critical but still a gap in the specific signal definition). Tailored support specific to the award type is implied through the CTU but not explicitly framed as 'award-specific' support.

evidence.text: social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. Total Staff Costs: £168,895.96 Dr Claire Hawcroft (Honorary Researcher, Univers

ity of Bristol) will lead on the distress protocol and advise on primary care referral pathways. Amanda Warburton (Honorary Researcher, UCL Psychiatry) will oversee recruitment of DA practitioners, develop the safeguarding protocol, and lead on safeguarding procedures throughout the trial. Non-Staff Costs 1. Data Capture, Randomisation, Training, and Participant Vouchers We have allocated costs for randomisation via Sealed Envelope (100 randomisations). REDCap will be used for data capture (annual fee waived for UCL). We have included training costs for our key outcome measures, and the fee for registering our trial (ISRCTN). All participants will receive £25 vouchers as recognition for their contribution. Total: £5,886.40 2. Dissemination We have included costs for a podcast to be co-produced with UCL Media, a final dissemination event, and attendance at a national conference. Total: £7,360.00 3. SoECAT Site-Level Research Costs As outlined in the SoECAT, these include research activities essential to study delivery, including recruitment, consent, and data collection. Total: £10,218.08 4. Estate, Infrastructure Technician Costs, and Other Indirect costs These amount to £143,023.4

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of lived experience integration. A Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) is central to co-designing focus groups, adapting the intervention, and interpreting data (secppi__001_b). Specific changes based on lived experience feedback are documented, including the selection of the 4-month follow-up timepoint and the revision of the Plain English Summary (seccfps__001_d, seccfps__001_e). A co-applicant with lived experience (Ms Marion Goodchild) is formally costed and leads the PPI strategy (secac__001).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the plan for adaptation is strong, the specific examples of changes made to the *initial* study design prior to this application are less detailed than the changes made in response to feedback. The evidence for wp.1.b is strong but relies heavily on the 'Changes from Previous Stage' section to demonstrate concrete design shifts.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the plan for adaptation is strong, the specific examples of changes made to the *initial* study design prior to this application are less detailed than the changes made in response to feedback. The evidence for wp.1.b is strong but relies heavily on the 'Changes from Previous Stage' section to demonstrate concrete design shifts.

evidence.text:
h will include three interlinked Work Streams, with a Lived
Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) contributing throughout.
Work Stream 1: We will conduct two survivor-led focus groups (n = 16) with older women affected by DA
to tailor the components of PATH to better meet the needs of older women. Practitioner focus groups (n
= 10) will further inform the adaptation of PATH, help identify training needs, and enhance practitioners’
ability to recognise the needs of older women.
Work Stream 2: Using individual interviews with survivors (n=10), and shadowing helpline phone calls
(n=10) through our partner Hourglass, we will explore the barriers older women face in accessing
support. We will work with frontline professionals and clinicians (n = 15) to identify and develop referral
pathways, that improve visibility and accessibility of services for older women in both primary care and
community settings.
Work Stream 3: We will test the feasibility and acceptability of A-PATH in a pilot RCT (n = 76 women),
by randomly allocating 38 women to DA advocacy and 38 to DA advocacy plus A-PATH. We will
measure rates of recruitment, randomisation, retention, and participant engagement with the
interve

for a general audience.
Thank you for this helpful feedback. We have revised the Plain English Summary to ensure it is clear,
and accessible. The revised version now explains in lay terms the purpose of the study, what the
research will involve, and how it could benefit older women affected by DA. We have involved members
of our Public and Patient Involvement (PPI) group, in reviewing the revised summary to ensure it is
understandable to a general audience. Our plain language summary has been developed with input
from our DA practitioner co-applicant, NIHR research design lay members, and experts by experience
consulted through our partner organisation, Hourglass.
7. The applicants should clarify the rationale for the 4-month follow-up being the main outcome
measurement point.
Thank you for this very important feedback. We consulted with lived experience experts to inform our
choice of primary outcome timepoint. They strongly emphasised the need to allow sufficient time for
meaningful and sustainable change to emerge given the complex, long-standing nature of DA in later
life. Their insights highlighted ensuring participants have adequate time to complete A-PATH and begin
applying

evidence.text:
th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by
dome

Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.
