FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a comprehensive dissemination plan including accessible reports, a public engagement event, and co-produced plain language summaries guided by public contributors (secsa__001_c, secppi__001_c). There are clear links between the study objectives (adapting PATH for older women) and anticipated benefits (improved safety, wellbeing, and future RCT evidence) (secpesor__001_c). The plan emphasizes actionable outcomes for policymakers and service providers.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the dissemination plan is strong, the specific mechanisms for ensuring the research outcomes directly translate into immediate improvements in health or care services (beyond informing a future trial) are somewhat prospective. The impact relies on the success of the feasibility study to justify the full trial.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the dissemination plan is strong, the specific mechanisms for ensuring the research outcomes directly translate into immediate improvements in health or care services (beyond informing a future trial) are somewhat prospective. The impact relies on the success of the feasibility study to justify the full trial.

evidence.text:
ntion. We will identify and administer measures which we think may be suitable as primary and
secondary outcomes in a full trial at baseline, 4, 8, and 12 months. This will include: psychological
distress, depression, and post-traumatic stress. We will assess the acceptability of collecting health-
related quality of life data to inform a future economic evaluation and conduct interviews with older
women and practitioners to explore lived experiences and contextual factors influencing outcomes.
We will work closely with community providers to engage women affected by multiple inequalities and
explore how these intersecting factors influence access to support services. Findings will be shared
through a series of accessible reports tailored for diverse audiences, alongside a public engagement
event to communicate key learning. If feasible, this study will provide the foundation for a future large
scale RCT evaluating the effectiveness of the adapted PATH, generating much needed evidence to
improve trauma-informed care and support for older women affected by DA.

rounds who have lived experience of abuse, have played a key
role in shaping the project, and will be involved throughout to ensure the programme meets everyone’s
needs. An older woman researcher with personal experience of domestic abuse is working closely with
us to make sure the study truly reflects what support older women need. The national charity Hourglass,
which works to end the abuse of older people, will help our study to ensure it is shaped by the needs
and voices of older women.
Knowledge mobilisation
When the study is finished, our public contributors will guide us in sharing what we have learned with the
women we worked with, as well as with doctors, nurses and organisations that support women affected
by abuse, including those who work with older women. If this small study testing the A-PATH
programme shows it could be helpful for older women, we will apply for further funding to carry out a
larger study with more people. Later, we hope to work with policymakers to help bring lasting
improvements in the well-being and safety of older women harmed by domestic abuse.

stic abuse.
We are committed to ensuring mutual benefit in all aspects of community involvement. Contributions will
be recognised through participation vouchers, flexible scheduling, and practical support to reduce
barriers such as digital exclusion. We will also offer lived experience experts opportunities for training in
research to support their ongoing development and meaningful involvement in the study (through
training in the PATH model and qualitative research methods).
Community members will receive letters of recognition to support future engagement in research. Our
LEAP, led by our peer researcher, will take a lead role in co-producing plain language summaries of our
findings for community dissemination. At the end of the project, we will host a public engagement event
with our partners (Hourglass, Age UK, Women’s Aid) to share results, raise awareness, and foster wider
social change. Rooted in co-production, this study will prioritise safe, inclusive participation to ensure the
research remains relevant, and respectful to the communities it serves.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets older women (60+) and those affected by multiple inequalities, a historically underserved group (secsa__001_a). It details specific partnerships with organizations supporting ethnically diverse communities (e.g., Shakti Women's Aid, Aanchal, London Black Women's Project) to ensure representation (secac__001). Recruitment strategies include culturally sensitive materials and co-design with community representatives (seccfps__001_b). Barriers such as digital exclusion and financial dependence are acknowledged and mitigated through vouchers and accessible venues (secppi__001_b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the strategies for recruitment are detailed, the application does not provide specific metrics or targets for the diversity of the final sample (e.g., percentage of ethnic minority participants), which would strengthen the evidence for wp.4.c.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the strategies for recruitment are detailed, the application does not provide specific metrics or targets for the diversity of the final sample (e.g., percentage of ethnic minority participants), which would strengthen the evidence for wp.4.c.

evidence.text:
Domestic abuse (DA) affects many women over the age of 60 and has a profound effect on their well-
being. Older women face distinct and often overlooked barriers to seeking help, such as ageist attitudes,
stigma and assumptions that DA does not happen in later life. Older women are more likely to be
financially dependent or live with the perpetrator, making it harder to access support and cope with the
abuse. Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing (PATH) is a trauma-informed intervention delivered by
DA practitioners that improves psychological outcomes in younger women. However, its effectiveness
for older women remains unclear.
In this programme of research, our aim is to adapt the PATH programme (A-PATH) to meet the unique
needs of older women, and assess its feasibility and acceptability for a future large scale randomised
controlled trial (RCT).
We will address two key research questions:
a) is it possible to adapt PATH in a way that is accessible to a diverse range of older women affected by
DA to empower and support their wellbeing, and recovery?
b) is it feasible and acceptable to deliver A-PATH in primary care, and community settings?
Our mixed-methods programme of researc

have established trust within ethnic minority communities to raise awareness of the study. Involving
community representatives in the co-design of recruitment strategies ensures they are responsive to
cultural needs. These measures will foster equitable access and participation from under-represented
groups, ultimately improving the relevance of the adapted PATH intervention.

4. The committee requires reassurance that the study can be delivered within the proposed time and
budget.
Thank you for your valuable feedback. We are confident in delivering the study within the proposed
timeframe and budget, supported by our proven track record. Our team has extensive experience
managing complex intervention studies in areas such as dementia, end-of-life care, and domestic
abuse, having led multiple trials with similar deadlines. We have strong partnerships with key
organisations (e.g., Hourglass, Women’s Aid, Age UK, SafeLives) that will actively support recruitment.
We will work closely with NHS Trusts that have demonstrated leadership in domestic abuse rese

practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by

FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: LEAP contributes to design (focus groups), conduct (data interpretation), and dissemination (plain language summaries) (secppi__001_a, secppi__001_b). Accessibility is considered through the use of safe, trusted venues and support for digital exclusion (secppi__001_b). The plan includes monitoring the strength of community engagement and retention of community voices (secppi__001_a).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation (wp.5.c) is present but relies on the LEAP convening at 'key milestones' rather than a continuous, iterative feedback loop described in detail. The specific mechanisms for adapting the study mid-stream based on inclusion metrics are less detailed than the initial design phase.
sub_criteria.rationale: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation (wp.5.c) is present but relies on the LEAP convening at 'key milestones' rather than a continuous, iterative feedback loop described in detail. The specific mechanisms for adapting the study mid-stream based on inclusion metrics are less detailed than the initial design phase.

evidence.text:
ntion. We will identify and administer measures which we think may be suitable as primary and
secondary outcomes in a full trial at baseline, 4, 8, and 12 months. This will include: psychological
distress, depression, and post-traumatic stress. We will assess the acceptability of collecting health-
related quality of life data to inform a future economic evaluation and conduct interviews with older
women and practitioners to explore lived experiences and contextual factors influencing outcomes.
We will work closely with community providers to engage women affected by multiple inequalities and
explore how these intersecting factors influence access to support services. Findings will be shared
through a series of accessible reports tailored for diverse audiences, alongside a public engagement
event to communicate key learning. If feasible, this study will provide the foundation for a future large
scale RCT evaluating the effectiveness of the adapted PATH, generating much needed evidence to
improve trauma-informed care and support for older women affected by DA.

We define our community as women aged 60 and over who have experienced domestic abuse, along
with their families, supporters, and the organisations that contribute to their recovery and well-being. Our
community-engaged research approach is grounded in the principles of equity, inclusivity, shared
learning, and collaborative decision-making, bringing together lived experience experts, practitioners,
third sector partners, clinicians and researchers.
We will work in close partnership with older women and the professionals who support them to co-
produce knowledge and promote meaningful involvement in all stages of the research. Our approach will
ensure that older women’s voices are central to shaping the intervention, and improving referral
pathways. We will evaluate the strength of our community engagement, by monitoring and measuring:
(1) the number and diversity of community contributors (e.g., local services, third sector organisations,
gatekeepers);
(2) engagement across the study, noting retention and consistency of community voices in shaping
study procedures, materials, and dissemination plans;
(3) the perceived impact of involvement, assessed through follow-up interviews wi

th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by
dome

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: The budget explicitly includes costs for the PPI strategy led by a co-applicant (Marion Goodchild, 20 days) (secsb__001_b). Participant vouchers (£25) are allocated to reduce barriers (secsb__001_c). Staff time is dedicated to inclusive data collection methods (blended phone/in-person) and training for lived experience experts (secsb__001_c). The alignment between the inclusion plan (LEAP, diverse recruitment) and the allocated resources (FTE for PPI lead, vouchers, training) is clear and well-justified.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion activities.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion activities.

evidence.text: nt) will oversee recruitment at the Swansea site and co-lead the WS3 qualitative process evaluation of lived experiences for survivors and practitioners (costed at 7% FTE for 24 months). Dr Roxane Agnew-Davies (co-applicant, Co-Lead of WS1) will lead on A-PATH training and supervision, contributing to the adaptation of training and A- PATH materials (costed for 57 days). Dr Alison Gregory (co-applicant, WS2 Co-Lead) will support the development of community referral pathways (WS2) and lead on third sector engagement partnerships with Women’s Aid and SafeLives (costed for 40 days). Dr Victoria Vickerstaff (statistical and health economics lead; co-applicant) will oversee statistical and economic evaluation (costed at 7.5% FTE for 24 months), supported by a junior researcher (employed by UCL). Marion Goodchild (lived experience co-applicant) will lead the PPI strategy and chair the Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). She will guide the adaptation of PATH, advise on recruitment, and review all public-facing materials (costed for 20 days). 2. Research Staff Employed by UCL We will employ a senior researcher (50% FTE for 22 months and 25% for 10 months; Grade 7) with a background in

social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of lived experience integration. A Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) is central to co-designing focus groups, adapting the intervention, and interpreting data (secppi__001_b). Specific changes based on lived experience feedback are documented, including the selection of the 4-month follow-up timepoint and the revision of the Plain English Summary (seccfps__001_d, seccfps__001_e). A co-applicant with lived experience (Ms Marion Goodchild) is formally costed and leads the PPI strategy (secac__001).
criteria.drawbacks: While the plan for adaptation is strong, the specific examples of changes made to the *initial* study design prior to this application are less detailed than the changes made in response to feedback. The evidence for wp.1.b is strong but relies heavily on the 'Changes from Previous Stage' section to demonstrate concrete design shifts.
criteria.rationale: While the plan for adaptation is strong, the specific examples of changes made to the *initial* study design prior to this application are less detailed than the changes made in response to feedback. The evidence for wp.1.b is strong but relies heavily on the 'Changes from Previous Stage' section to demonstrate concrete design shifts.

evidence.text:
h will include three interlinked Work Streams, with a Lived
Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) contributing throughout.
Work Stream 1: We will conduct two survivor-led focus groups (n = 16) with older women affected by DA
to tailor the components of PATH to better meet the needs of older women. Practitioner focus groups (n
= 10) will further inform the adaptation of PATH, help identify training needs, and enhance practitioners’
ability to recognise the needs of older women.
Work Stream 2: Using individual interviews with survivors (n=10), and shadowing helpline phone calls
(n=10) through our partner Hourglass, we will explore the barriers older women face in accessing
support. We will work with frontline professionals and clinicians (n = 15) to identify and develop referral
pathways, that improve visibility and accessibility of services for older women in both primary care and
community settings.
Work Stream 3: We will test the feasibility and acceptability of A-PATH in a pilot RCT (n = 76 women),
by randomly allocating 38 women to DA advocacy and 38 to DA advocacy plus A-PATH. We will
measure rates of recruitment, randomisation, retention, and participant engagement with the
interve

for a general audience.
Thank you for this helpful feedback. We have revised the Plain English Summary to ensure it is clear,
and accessible. The revised version now explains in lay terms the purpose of the study, what the
research will involve, and how it could benefit older women affected by DA. We have involved members
of our Public and Patient Involvement (PPI) group, in reviewing the revised summary to ensure it is
understandable to a general audience. Our plain language summary has been developed with input
from our DA practitioner co-applicant, NIHR research design lay members, and experts by experience
consulted through our partner organisation, Hourglass.
7. The applicants should clarify the rationale for the 4-month follow-up being the main outcome
measurement point.
Thank you for this very important feedback. We consulted with lived experience experts to inform our
choice of primary outcome timepoint. They strongly emphasised the need to allow sufficient time for
meaningful and sustainable change to emerge given the complex, long-standing nature of DA in later
life. Their insights highlighted ensuring participants have adequate time to complete A-PATH and begin
applying

evidence.text:
th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by
dome

criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The research team includes a co-applicant with lived experience (Ms Marion Goodchild) and a diverse mix of academic and practitioner roles (secac__001). The team composition reflects a blend of skills (psychology, social work, statistics, domestic abuse expertise) which supports equitable task allocation (secsb__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: There is no explicit mention of the team's demographic diversity (e.g., ethnicity, gender balance, age) beyond the inclusion of one lived experience co-applicant. No specific statements regarding inclusion in hiring or team-building strategies are provided. The evidence for wp.2.a and wp.2.b is weak as the application focuses on participant diversity rather than team diversity.
criteria.rationale: There is no explicit mention of the team's demographic diversity (e.g., ethnicity, gender balance, age) beyond the inclusion of one lived experience co-applicant. No specific statements regarding inclusion in hiring or team-building strategies are provided. FUNDING COMMITTEE FEEDBACK:
Feedback 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. Response to feedback (completed by the research team) Response to feedback Thank you for this helpful feedback to our application. Please see below our detailed response. 1. The applicants should be explicit about whether a theory of change model is going to be used given that this was suggested in the research specification. Thank you for this important point. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: The application details a comprehensive dissemination plan including accessible reports, a public engagement event, and co-produced plain language summaries guided by public contributors (secsa__001_c, secppi__001_c). There are clear links between the study objectives (adapting PATH for older women) and anticipated benefits (improved safety, wellbeing, and future RCT evidence) (secpesor__001_c). The plan emphasizes actionable outcomes for policymakers and service providers.
criteria.drawbacks: While the dissemination plan is strong, the specific mechanisms for ensuring the research outcomes directly translate into immediate improvements in health or care services (beyond informing a future trial) are somewhat prospective. The impact relies on the success of the feasibility study to justify the full trial.
criteria.rationale: While the dissemination plan is strong, the specific mechanisms for ensuring the research outcomes directly translate into immediate improvements in health or care services (beyond informing a future trial) are somewhat prospective. The impact relies on the success of the feasibility study to justify the full trial.

evidence.text:
ntion. We will identify and administer measures which we think may be suitable as primary and
secondary outcomes in a full trial at baseline, 4, 8, and 12 months. This will include: psychological
distress, depression, and post-traumatic stress. We will assess the acceptability of collecting health-
related quality of life data to inform a future economic evaluation and conduct interviews with older
women and practitioners to explore lived experiences and contextual factors influencing outcomes.
We will work closely with community providers to engage women affected by multiple inequalities and
explore how these intersecting factors influence access to support services. Findings will be shared
through a series of accessible reports tailored for diverse audiences, alongside a public engagement
event to communicate key learning. If feasible, this study will provide the foundation for a future large
scale RCT evaluating the effectiveness of the adapted PATH, generating much needed evidence to
improve trauma-informed care and support for older women affected by DA.

rounds who have lived experience of abuse, have played a key
role in shaping the project, and will be involved throughout to ensure the programme meets everyone’s
needs. An older woman researcher with personal experience of domestic abuse is working closely with
us to make sure the study truly reflects what support older women need. The national charity Hourglass,
which works to end the abuse of older people, will help our study to ensure it is shaped by the needs
and voices of older women.
Knowledge mobilisation
When the study is finished, our public contributors will guide us in sharing what we have learned with the
women we worked with, as well as with doctors, nurses and organisations that support women affected
by abuse, including those who work with older women. If this small study testing the A-PATH
programme shows it could be helpful for older women, we will apply for further funding to carry out a
larger study with more people. Later, we hope to work with policymakers to help bring lasting
improvements in the well-being and safety of older women harmed by domestic abuse.

stic abuse.
We are committed to ensuring mutual benefit in all aspects of community involvement. Contributions will
be recognised through participation vouchers, flexible scheduling, and practical support to reduce
barriers such as digital exclusion. We will also offer lived experience experts opportunities for training in
research to support their ongoing development and meaningful involvement in the study (through
training in the PATH model and qualitative research methods).
Community members will receive letters of recognition to support future engagement in research. Our
LEAP, led by our peer researcher, will take a lead role in co-producing plain language summaries of our
findings for community dissemination. At the end of the project, we will host a public engagement event
with our partners (Hourglass, Age UK, Women’s Aid) to share results, raise awareness, and foster wider
social change. Rooted in co-production, this study will prioritise safe, inclusive participation to ensure the
research remains relevant, and respectful to the communities it serves.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets older women (60+) and those affected by multiple inequalities, a historically underserved group (secsa__001_a). It details specific partnerships with organizations supporting ethnically diverse communities (e.g., Shakti Women's Aid, Aanchal, London Black Women's Project) to ensure representation (secac__001). Recruitment strategies include culturally sensitive materials and co-design with community representatives (seccfps__001_b). Barriers such as digital exclusion and financial dependence are acknowledged and mitigated through vouchers and accessible venues (secppi__001_b).
criteria.drawbacks: While the strategies for recruitment are detailed, the application does not provide specific metrics or targets for the diversity of the final sample (e.g., percentage of ethnic minority participants), which would strengthen the evidence for wp.4.c.
criteria.rationale: While the strategies for recruitment are detailed, the application does not provide specific metrics or targets for the diversity of the final sample (e.g., percentage of ethnic minority participants), which would strengthen the evidence for wp.4.c.

evidence.text:
Domestic abuse (DA) affects many women over the age of 60 and has a profound effect on their well-
being. Older women face distinct and often overlooked barriers to seeking help, such as ageist attitudes,
stigma and assumptions that DA does not happen in later life. Older women are more likely to be
financially dependent or live with the perpetrator, making it harder to access support and cope with the
abuse. Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing (PATH) is a trauma-informed intervention delivered by
DA practitioners that improves psychological outcomes in younger women. However, its effectiveness
for older women remains unclear.
In this programme of research, our aim is to adapt the PATH programme (A-PATH) to meet the unique
needs of older women, and assess its feasibility and acceptability for a future large scale randomised
controlled trial (RCT).
We will address two key research questions:
a) is it possible to adapt PATH in a way that is accessible to a diverse range of older women affected by
DA to empower and support their wellbeing, and recovery?
b) is it feasible and acceptable to deliver A-PATH in primary care, and community settings?
Our mixed-methods programme of researc

have established trust within ethnic minority communities to raise awareness of the study. Involving
community representatives in the co-design of recruitment strategies ensures they are responsive to
cultural needs. These measures will foster equitable access and participation from under-represented
groups, ultimately improving the relevance of the adapted PATH intervention.

Thank you for your valuable feedback. We are confident in delivering the study within the proposed

abuse, having led multiple trials with similar deadlines. We have strong partnerships with key
organisations (e.g., Hourglass, Women’s Aid, Age UK, SafeLives) that will actively support recruitment.
We will work closely with NHS Trusts that have demonstrated leadership in domestic abuse rese

evidence.text:
th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by

We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. She will join the Priment CTU Steering Group (https://www.ucl.ac.uk/priment/priment-steering-group), present progress at regular meetings, and receive ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations to ensure methodological rigour and effective delivery. The lead applicant will also attend regular departmental meetings for Chief Investigators of clinical trials. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. The committee notes that the above points will lead to an increase in costs, however this should be limited to a modest increase. Thank you for this observation. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. In addition, we will draw on the local resources and expertise available at each study site to strengthen engagement and inclusion. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. The committee suggests that a suitable minimum number of participants is set for the focus group work and interviews to ensure that they produce a meaningful output. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We have now specified minimum numbers to ensure meaningful outputs: Work Stream 1 – Adaptation of PATH • Each focus group with survivors will include 6–8 participants to ensure meaningful input into exploring the intervention content and adapting it to the needs of older women. • Each focus group with domestic abuse practitioners will include 6–8 participants to assess and adapt the original training package. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. Collecting 12-month data is particularly valuable for this group, as older women affected by domestic abuse may experience complex and evolving needs over time. Tracking retention at this stage will help us understand factors that influence continued engagement, identify barriers to sustained support, and provide insights into the longer-term relevance and acceptability of the intervention within this population. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers’ comments. All of the proposed changes will be accommodated. We have also written to the Women’s Institute and hope to arrange a meeting with them very soon including local representatives. In addition, we will engage with relevant contacts in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the East Midlands to ensure broad input and representation in the project. References Cluss PA, Chang JC, Hawker L, Scholle SH, Dado D, Buranosky R, Goldstrohm S. The process of change for victims of intimate partner violence: Support for a psychosocial readiness model. Women Health Iss. 2006;16(5):262-74. Other changes to the application No changes have been made to the application because of other factors.
