criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: LEAP contributes to design (focus groups), conduct (data interpretation), and dissemination (plain language summaries) (secppi__001_a, secppi__001_b). Accessibility is considered through the use of safe, trusted venues and support for digital exclusion (secppi__001_b). The plan includes monitoring the strength of community engagement and retention of community voices (secppi__001_a).
criteria.drawbacks: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation (wp.5.c) is present but relies on the LEAP convening at 'key milestones' rather than a continuous, iterative feedback loop described in detail. The specific mechanisms for adapting the study mid-stream based on inclusion metrics are less detailed than the initial design phase.
criteria.rationale: The plan for ongoing evaluation and adaptation (wp.5.c) is present but relies on the LEAP convening at 'key milestones' rather than a continuous, iterative feedback loop described in detail. The specific mechanisms for adapting the study mid-stream based on inclusion metrics are less detailed than the initial design phase.

evidence.text:
ntion. We will identify and administer measures which we think may be suitable as primary and
secondary outcomes in a full trial at baseline, 4, 8, and 12 months. This will include: psychological
distress, depression, and post-traumatic stress. We will assess the acceptability of collecting health-
related quality of life data to inform a future economic evaluation and conduct interviews with older
women and practitioners to explore lived experiences and contextual factors influencing outcomes.
We will work closely with community providers to engage women affected by multiple inequalities and
explore how these intersecting factors influence access to support services. Findings will be shared
through a series of accessible reports tailored for diverse audiences, alongside a public engagement
event to communicate key learning. If feasible, this study will provide the foundation for a future large
scale RCT evaluating the effectiveness of the adapted PATH, generating much needed evidence to
improve trauma-informed care and support for older women affected by DA.

We define our community as women aged 60 and over who have experienced domestic abuse, along
with their families, supporters, and the organisations that contribute to their recovery and well-being. Our
community-engaged research approach is grounded in the principles of equity, inclusivity, shared
learning, and collaborative decision-making, bringing together lived experience experts, practitioners,
third sector partners, clinicians and researchers.
We will work in close partnership with older women and the professionals who support them to co-
produce knowledge and promote meaningful involvement in all stages of the research. Our approach will
ensure that older women’s voices are central to shaping the intervention, and improving referral
pathways. We will evaluate the strength of our community engagement, by monitoring and measuring:
(1) the number and diversity of community contributors (e.g., local services, third sector organisations,
gatekeepers);
(2) engagement across the study, noting retention and consistency of community voices in shaping
study procedures, materials, and dissemination plans;
(3) the perceived impact of involvement, assessed through follow-up interviews wi

th older women and
practitioners.
From the earliest stages of our study design, we engaged a core group of older women with lived
experience, who form our Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). This panel will collaborate with the
research team to co-design focus group agendas, participate in facilitation, and play a central role in
interpreting study data. The LEAP will be chaired by our co-applicant with lived experience, who will help
shape the group’s focus and priorities over the course of the study. The LEAP group will convene at key
milestones of the project to contribute to:
a) adapting PATH content;
b) identifying referral pathways and potential barriers to access;
c) refining the core principles underpinning the intervention;
d) help to plan and guide the feasibility study.
To support participation, we will use safe, accessible, and trusted local venues such as Age UK centres,
women’s groups, and GP practices to host focus groups, LEAP meetings, and PATH sessions. Informed
by Goodman et al.’s 2017 community engagement toolkit, we will ensure that all materials are
accessible, culturally appropriate and relevant to the preferences and needs of older women affected by
dome

criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

criteria.pros: The budget explicitly includes costs for the PPI strategy led by a co-applicant (Marion Goodchild, 20 days) (secsb__001_b). Participant vouchers (£25) are allocated to reduce barriers (secsb__001_c). Staff time is dedicated to inclusive data collection methods (blended phone/in-person) and training for lived experience experts (secsb__001_c). The alignment between the inclusion plan (LEAP, diverse recruitment) and the allocated resources (FTE for PPI lead, vouchers, training) is clear and well-justified.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion activities.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the budgeting for inclusion is comprehensive and directly supports the stated inclusion activities.

Dr Roxane Agnew-Davies (co-applicant, Co-Lead of WS1) will lead on A-PATH training and supervision, contributing to the adaptation of training and A- PATH materials (costed for 57 days). Dr Alison Gregory (co-applicant, WS2 Co-Lead) will support the development of community referral pathways (WS2) and lead on third sector engagement partnerships with Women’s Aid and SafeLives (costed for 40 days). Dr Victoria Vickerstaff (statistical and health economics lead; co-applicant) will oversee statistical and economic evaluation (costed at 7.5% FTE for 24 months), supported by a junior researcher (employed by UCL). Marion Goodchild (lived experience co-applicant) will lead the PPI strategy and chair the Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP). She will guide the adaptation of PATH, advise on recruitment, and review all public-facing materials (costed for 20 days). 2. Research Staff Employed by UCL We will employ a senior researcher (50% FTE for 22 months and 25% for 10 months; Grade 7) with a background in

social work and expertise in domestic abuse and safeguarding older adults. This role will also help build early-career research capacity, with the post-holder undertaking a structured training plan and receiving mentorship by senior leaders in the field. Managed by Dr Orgeta and Dr Cramer, they will support recruitment and data collection across all Work Streams and assist WS Leads with project coordination. From our experience data collection will primarily be conducted over the phone to prioritise accessibility, flexibility, and participant comfort. Where feasible and appropriate, we will also offer in- person data collection through local Clinical Research Network (CRN) sites to support participants who prefer face-to-face engagement or need additional assistance. This blended approach aims to promote inclusivity and optimise recruitment and retention. Dr Phuong Leung (Senior Research Fellow, UCL), who led the previous lived experience study, will co- lead the process evaluation interviews of WP3 (10% FTE over 16 months) alongside Professor Wydall and support final dissemination of academic outputs. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. 8. The applicants should incorporate the changes proposed in response to the external reviewers' comments. In adapting APATH (Adapting Psychological Advocacy Towards Healing), we will draw on the Psychosocial Readiness Model (PRM) of change (Cluss et al., 2006). This model is well suited to older women affected by lifelong abuse, as it recognises how psychological, situational, and social factors influence readiness for change, and provides a framework to tailor our intervention so that it promotes empowerment, engagement, and sustained recovery. We will refer to the theory of change at the start of the study as we adapt our intervention and set the research up. We will return to the theory of change when we have collected our data. If the pilot has shown acceptability and feasibility for a full trial, we will revise the theory of change in preparation for our full trial application. 2. The current timeline is overly ambitious and should be reconsidered. We have reviewed and adjusted the project timeline, extending it by six months to ensure feasibility (full details appear on our Revised Time plan for the study). This revised timeframe will allow us to collect 12-month follow-up data for all participants and support the thorough completion of all project activities. 3. The project would benefit from the involvement of a clinical trials unit that can provide relevant delivery expertise. Thank you for this helpful suggestion. We agree that clinical trials expertise will strengthen the project. The lead applicant will work closely with UCL’s Priment Clinical Trials Unit (CTU), which will provide guidance on study design, data management, and delivery processes. In addition, our team includes four academics (VO, HC, VV, RAD) with prior experience in the delivery and management of NIHR-funded clinical trials in areas such as dementia, domestic abuse, and end- of-life care. 4. We recognise that the revisions, including the extended timeframe result in a cost increase; however, we have been very careful to limit this to a modest increase, ensuring that all additional costs are fully justified and essential to delivering a high-quality study. 5. Further consideration of how the inclusion of a diverse range of participants can be ensured so that different groups are represented. Thank you for this important point. We are committed to ensuring the inclusion of a diverse range of participants to reflect the varied experiences of older women affected by domestic abuse. Recruitment will be supported through community and voluntary sector organisations, including Hourglass, Women’s Aid, and groups representing ethnically and culturally diverse communities. As part of this project, we also aim to develop a community network specialising in supporting diverse groups—such as Shakti Women’s Aid, The Angelou Centre, and the London Black Women’s Project—to enhance inclusivity and ensure the intervention is relevant and accessible to all participants. We will also work closely with Aanchal, who support South Asian women affected by domestic abuse. For example, in Bristol, we will collaborate with local health inequality community champions, including the BNSSG ICB Health and Social Care Research Ambassadors and the new Deep End Health Inequality Fellows. These roles are specifically designed to support research studies in increasing diversity and inclusion, and their involvement will help ensure that recruitment strategies are locally grounded and responsive to the needs of underrepresented groups. 6. Work Stream 2 – Developing referral pathways • We will conduct a minimum of 10 interviews with women with lived experience to understand barriers and facilitators to accessing services. • Each stakeholder focus group will include 6–8 participants to explore the suitability of current referral pathways. Work Stream 3 – Follow-up interviews of A-PATH • We will conduct a minimum of 15 interviews with women with lived experience to understand their experiences of A-PATH and identify areas for improvement. We will interview a minimum of 10 women that receive usual care. • We will conduct 6–8 interviews with domestic abuse practitioners delivering the intervention. This approach will provide sufficient diversity and depth to capture a broad range of perspectives. We will also monitor data saturation throughout the data collection process to ensure that sampling continues until no new themes or insights emerge, thereby strengthening the robustness and credibility of the findings. 7. Data on retention at 12 months should be collected. Thank you for this important point. We will collect retention data at 12 months for all participants to monitor engagement, assess the feasibility of sustained participation, and inform the long-term evaluation of the intervention. sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application form analysis confirms the presence of 7 distinct sections with 10 numbered heading-like lines and 22 list markers, indicating a strong, consistent hierarchy (af.1.a, af.1.c). The parser detected 69 emphasis markers, suggesting effective use of bolding or emphasis for key terms (af.1.b), and the structural analysis itself serves as direct evidence of detected formatting patterns (af.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While 69 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during extraction; thus, the score for emphasis (af.1.b) is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.
sub_criteria.rationale: While 69 emphasis markers were detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during extraction; thus, the score for emphasis (af.1.b) is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.

findings.implication: Scientific Abstract: Clearly outlines problem, objectives, methods, and beneficiaries, supporting pr.1.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies a knowledge gap regarding PATH effectiveness for older women, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Defines a feasible pilot RCT scope (n=76) and specific work streams, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Details mixed-methods design, randomization, and outcome measures, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights societal impact on older women's wellbeing and future RCT foundation, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions Lived Experience Advisory Panel (LEAP) and survivor-led focus groups, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Focuses on diverse older women and intersecting inequalities, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Describes LEAP contribution and survivor-led focus groups for co-design, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Plans accessible reports and public engagement events to share findings, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Targets older women and those affected by multiple inequalities, supporting wp.4.
Scientific Abstract: Integrates lived experience in design, recruitment, and outcome exploration, supporting wp.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates the problem, aims, methods (randomized groups), beneficiaries, and impact in accessible language, strongly supporting pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies a specific gap where current services target younger women and fail older women, supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes a feasible pilot study with a small group and specific intervention duration (8-12 weeks), supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly details a randomized controlled trial design with control and intervention groups, supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Highlights direct benefits for older women's safety and well-being, and plans for policy influence, supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes involvement of women with lived experience in shaping the project and an older woman researcher, supporting pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly mentions working with older women of different ages and backgrounds to ensure inclusivity, supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: States that women with lived experience played a key role in shaping the project, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines plans to share findings with participants, health professionals, and policymakers, supporting wp.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Focuses specifically on older women (60+) and diverse backgrounds, addressing an underserved group, supporting wp.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Indicates involvement of public contributors throughout the project lifecycle, supporting wp.5.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants revised the Plain English Summary for clarity and accessibility, involving PPI and lay members to ensure it is understandable to a general audience.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants corrected the timeline and expanded to ten sites with an eight-month recruitment period, demonstrating feasibility and alignment with award scope.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants clarified retention investigation, adherence definitions, and sample size calculations, strengthening methodological rigor and justification.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants detailed inclusive recruitment strategies for non-English speaking and ethnic communities, including culturally sensitive materials and community partnerships.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants integrated lived experience feedback into intervention development, plain language summaries, and outcome timepoint selection.
Changes from Previous Stage: Applicants explicitly addressed recruitment of under-represented groups through co-design with community representatives and partnerships with frontline services.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details LEAP co-design of focus groups, data interpretation, and intervention adaptation, strongly supporting wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes co-produced plain language summaries and public engagement events to share results, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly targets older women (60+) with domestic abuse experience and uses accessible venues to mitigate barriers, supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines involvement across all stages (design, data interpretation, dissemination) with monitoring of retention and impact, supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes a robust Lived Experience Advisory Panel and co-production approach, reinforcing pr.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Mentions accessible venues, culturally appropriate materials, and support for digital exclusion, supporting pr.9.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed cost breakdowns for staff, data capture, dissemination, and site costs, explicitly linking resources to project tasks and scope.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details specific supervisory roles (Dr Orgeta, Dr Cramer) and mentorship for a junior researcher, demonstrating structured support arrangements.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Lists allocated costs for estate, infrastructure, and technician support, indicating organizational resources available for project delivery.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes specific budget lines for PPI strategy (Marion Goodchild), participant vouchers, and inclusive data collection methods, supporting a costed inclusion plan.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Mentions a structured training plan and mentorship for a senior researcher role, indicating relevance to project methods.
Application Context: Lists team members with prior experience in NIHR-funded clinical trials in dementia and domestic abuse, supporting g.3.
Application Context: Describes lead applicants as Chief Co-lead investigators overseeing project management and recruitment, indicating increasing responsibility, supporting g.5.
Application Context: Responds to feedback by extending the timeline by six months to ensure feasibility and collecting 12-month data, supporting pr.3.
Application Context: Details collaboration with UCL's Priment Clinical Trials Unit for study design and data management, supporting pr.4.
Application Context: Outlines specific strategies to recruit diverse participants via community organizations like Shakti Women's Aid and Aanchal, supporting pr.9.
Application Context: Mentions team members with prior experience in NIHR-funded trials, indicating a track record of the contracting organization and partners, supporting ss.1.
Application Context: Describes lead applicant joining the Priment CTU Steering Group and receiving ongoing support from the Head of Clinical Trials Operations, supporting ss.2.
Application Context: Confirms access to UCL's Priment Clinical Trials Unit for guidance on study design and delivery, supporting ss.3.
Application Context: Includes a PPIE co-applicant (Ms Marion Goodchild) leading the engagement strategy and overseeing changes to training materials, supporting wp.1.
Application Context: Explicitly lists partnerships with organizations supporting ethnically diverse communities and South Asian women, supporting wp.4.
Application Context: Allocates 10% FTE to a PPIE lead and mentions developing a community network, indicating a resourced plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
